title: Learn AWS IAM in less than 10 minutes
summary: A tutorial video explaining AWS Identity and Access Management fundamentals for beginners. Covers users, roles, policies, least privilege principle, IAM Access Analyzer, and security best practices to avoid common access errors.
writeup: This nine-minute AWS primer dissects the access denied error that nearly every new user patches with administrator access, and explains why that reflex is a serious security failure. The video walks through the three load-bearing IAM concepts (users, roles, policies), illustrates the difference with concrete analogies (employee badge versus visitor pass), and builds to the principle of least privilege with a real Lambda-to-S3 example. The through-line is that the error message is a precise signal, not an obstacle: reading it correctly makes guessing unnecessary.
key_points: Attaching administrator access to silence an access denied error gives the identity permission to delete databases, spin up costly resources, and read every piece of data in the account, because it needed exactly one action on one bucket.

IAM users carry long-term credentials and represent humans logging into the console or CLI; IAM roles carry short-lived, auto-expiring credentials and are the correct identity for services such as Lambda functions and EC2 instances.

A policy is a JSON document that must explicitly grant every permission; without an attached policy an IAM identity is denied everything by default, so the correct fix for access denied is reading the error and granting only the specific action and resource named.

The least privilege principle limits blast radius: a Lambda function scoped to s3:GetObject on a single bucket, if compromised, can only read objects from that bucket rather than touching the entire account.

IAM Access Analyzer observes what an identity actually calls over a period of time and generates a scoped policy from that real activity, removing the excuse that writing precise policies is too much work up front.

Access keys must never appear in source code, config files, or environment variables committed to git; bots scan public repositories for exposed keys within minutes, so roles with temporary credentials are the correct pattern for any code that runs on AWS.
